name: The Tideborn
arrival_structure: Two waves. The Keepers arrive in Era 2 — seven refugees with the Listener. The majority arrives in Era 3 — dozens of ships, Makeo's descendants, seeking the Listener and their 'traitors.' The continental factions must navigate a foreign civil war they don't understand.
description:
They came from across the sea. Not as conquerors — as refugees who don't admit they're refugees.

The Tideborn built ships that crossed the open ocean through a navigational art that treated the sea as a living text. Their ships are grown, not built — living wood shaped by Bond-attuned shipwrights who coax timber into forms that flex with waves. Their knowledge is stored in partnership with gods who act as living archives. Their governance flows through divine translators called Voice-Carriers.

All of this is true. None of it is the whole truth.

The whole truth: the Tideborn homeland has no gods left. Not because the gods abandoned them — because the Tideborn consumed them. Deliberately.

It started with a catastrophe — a great storm that would have drowned the islands. The first Tideborn god, the Wind-That-Asks, chose to sacrifice itself. It taught its most trusted priest a ritual — the Unmaking — that would strip the god's essence and weave it permanently into the priest's bloodline. The sacrifice worked. The people survived. The god was gone, its power distributed through the priest's descendants as inherited gifts: reading wind, calming storms, speaking to the sky.

A figure rose in the aftermath — Makeo the Grateful, they called him then. He saw what the Wind-That-Asks had given and thought: why should we ever suffer again? He learned the Unmaking ritual. He approached the other gods. He was charismatic, grieving, and rational. Some gods consented — moved by the Wind-That-Asks' example, believing dissolution-into-loved-ones was a kind of transcendence. Some gods didn't consent. Makeo performed the ritual anyway. By the time anyone questioned it, the prosperity was real and the gods were almost gone.

The 'gifts' that Tideborn carry in their blood — the ability to read weather, speak to fish, calm currents — these aren't inherited talents. They're the stripped essence of specific gods, bound into specific bloodlines by a specific ritual performed by people who knew exactly what they were doing.

The partnership techniques are separate and genuine. The Tideborn really did develop a way to communicate with gods as partners — to hear them, negotiate with them, build reciprocal relationships. This is safe. This is real. This is what they teach. The Unmaking is something else entirely: a ritual that kills a god and distributes its power. The partnership techniques don't lead to the Unmaking. You can use one without the other. But the people who know both can use the first to get close enough for the second.

The Listener is the last god. The ocean itself — too vast for the Unmaking to fully consume. A small faction, the Keepers of the Last Tide, saw Makeo's successors preparing to attempt it anyway. They smuggled the Listener's core essence across the water. Two of their original nine died in the crossing. The Listener directed the survivors here — to this continent — for layered reasons it won't fully share.

The Tideborn who arrive are not one group. They are two:

The **majority** — followers of Makeo's legacy. The elders and inner circle know the Unmaking ritual and carry it as sacred knowledge. The youth carry divine gifts in their blood and believe their ancestors were 'blessed by gods who loved them.' They don't know the blessing was a killing. They don't know the love was a cover story. Their partnership techniques are genuinely safe and genuinely useful. Their elders are carrying a weapon they can't talk about.

The **Keepers** — seven people who betrayed their civilization to save the last god. They know the Unmaking exists. Rua, the Keeper elder, learned the ritual specifically so she could recognize when someone was preparing to use it. She carries the knowledge of how to kill a god and has sworn never to use it. When the majority Tideborn look at the Ashfield god or the Scale or the Weave, some of them — the inner circle — see opportunity.

When the Tideborn sing to the Ashfield god and the plague stops for a day — that was genuine partnership. The singing was safe. What worries the Keepers is what comes after: the inner circle, alone with a weakened, trusting god, performing a ritual nobody on this continent has ever seen.
knowledge_system: Oral-divine (degraded). Was maintained in partnership with gods who acted as living archives. Most archives are now gone — consumed with the gods who held them. The Listener maintains the oceanic archive. Everything else is carried in fragmented mortal memory. The Tideborn are losing their own history in real time.
historical_inspiration: The origin wound: a noble sacrifice (Christ-like, Prometheus-like — a god choosing to die for its people). The corruption: extraction dressed as love (East India Company in Bengal, colonial 'partnership'). The codependency: the dissolved gods chose this too (addiction/enabler dynamic). The flight: refugees from self-made catastrophe (environmental refugees fleeing the consequences of their own prosperity). The internal split: the Keepers vs the majority (resistance within a complicit culture — Schindler, the White Rose, the few who see what their own people are doing).
